# The ASI Stack {.unnumbered}

**The ASI Stack: A Systems Architecture for Governed, Efficient, Self-Improving AI** is a living technical book by Corben Sorenson.

![Layered conceptual architecture for the ASI Stack](assets/images/asi-stack-hero.png){#fig-asi-stack-hero fig-alt="A light technical illustration of a layered transparent systems stack with routing nodes, ledgers, and controlled feedback loops."}

## Current status

::: {.living-dashboard}
<div>
<strong>Book state</strong>
<span>v1.0.0 living-book release; v1.x evidence and reader-quality work active</span>
</div>
<div>
<strong>Sources</strong>
<span>194 public-safe records; 44/44 chapters externally positioned</span>
</div>
<div>
<strong>Claims</strong>
<span>44 chapter core claims remain argument-level</span>
</div>
<div>
<strong>Tests</strong>
<span>Three narrow non-core evidence transitions accepted; no chapter-core promotion</span>
</div>
:::

## 60-second trust surface

**What this is.** This is a public living-book research program for an ASI systems architecture. Its strongest current contribution is the evidence discipline: manifest-driven chapters, source queues, claim/support-state separation, Lean hooks, schemas, harnesses, release records, and reader-edition gates.

**What this is not.** This is not a validated ASI implementation, deployed safety system, benchmark-proven architecture, or claim that the chapter theses are already externally proven.

**What is currently evidenced.** All 44 chapter core claims remain at `argument`. Three narrow non-core transitions are accepted in [`docs/non_core_evidence_ledger.md`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/asi-stack-book/blob/main/docs/non_core_evidence_ledger.md): the Phase 5 harness registry runner and the costed-route/resource-budget slice are `synthetic-test-backed`, and one bounded Circle rope receipt replay is `prototype-backed`. Lean coverage is broad but still shallow in safety-critical modules. All 44 chapters now have source-noted external positioning records with 0 explicit external-baseline exceptions; that is a credibility and routing surface, not exhaustive literature synthesis or support-state promotion. Circle now also has a public ASI consumer-gate fixture checked by digest and negative controls in [`docs/circle_public_replay_consumer_gate.md`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/asi-stack-book/blob/main/docs/circle_public_replay_consumer_gate.md), but it is a guard around receipt use, not a fourth accepted transition. Project Theseus has one public-safe static architecture-gate report import checked by digest in [`docs/theseus_report_import_slice.md`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/asi-stack-book/blob/main/docs/theseus_report_import_slice.md), but this is implementation-reference evidence only and not a chapter-core promotion. Independent external human review is requested in [GitHub issue #1](https://github.com/corbensorenson/asi-stack-book/issues/1), but no review has been accepted as evidence.

## Core thesis

Efficient ASI should not be treated as one giant opaque model. It should be treated as a governed cognitive stack: alignment, governance, planning, memory, reasoning, execution, routing, compression, evidence, and recursive self-improvement cooperating through typed boundaries, verification, artifact memory, and bounded authority.

The central design move is simple: never let intelligence, authority, memory, evidence, and execution collapse into one undifferentiated loop. A capable system can generate, plan, retrieve, reason, route, and act; a governed system also records which layer had permission to do each thing, which artifact carried the decision, which evidence justified it, and which rollback path remains available.

## Architecture at a glance

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Intent["Human intent"] --> Constitution["Alignment and constitution"]
  Constitution --> Authority["Governance and authority"]
  Authority --> Plan["Planning and semantic IR"]
  Plan --> Memory["Virtual context memory"]
  Memory --> Route["Routing and specialist cores"]
  Route --> Reason["Reasoning, claims, and verification"]
  Reason --> Execute["Labor OS and runtime adapters"]
  Execute --> Artifacts["Artifacts, audit logs, and replay"]
  Artifacts --> Evidence["Evidence states and benchmark ratchets"]
  Evidence --> Improve["Governed self-improvement"]
  Improve --> Authority

  Authority -. "permission ceilings" .-> Execute
  Memory -. "source-bound packets" .-> Reason
  Evidence -. "promotion or rollback" .-> Route
  Evidence -. "qualification records" .-> Improve
```

Every arrow is meant to become an interface, not just a metaphor. The book is organized so each chapter owns one boundary: what enters it, what artifact it emits, what invariants must hold, what can fail, and what evidence would justify stronger claims.

## Dependency spine

The chapter list is dynamic, but the argument should remain stable when chapters are inserted, moved, split, or merged. The living spine is the dependency order below: governance defines what may happen, operational layers lower intent into work, evidence decides what can improve, and the release layer keeps the book itself honest.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Unit["Stack as unit of analysis"] --> AuthorityModel["Boundaries, authority, and failures"]
  AuthorityModel --> EvidenceLanguage["Evidence states and claim discipline"]
  EvidenceLanguage --> IntentLayer["Human intent, constitution, agency, and value conflict"]
  IntentLayer --> ChangeRights["Governance rights, stable fields, replacement, security, and RSI gates"]
  ChangeRights --> ControlSpine["Intent contracts, commands, plans, semantic IR, and context ABI"]
  ControlSpine --> EpistemicSpine["Context adequacy, claim ledgers, proof-carrying claims, and tribunal review"]
  EpistemicSpine --> ExecutionSpine["Typed jobs, artifacts, adapters, and procedural memory"]
  ExecutionSpine --> CapabilitySpine["Routing, readiness gates, MoECOT, and personal compute hives"]
  CapabilitySpine --> EfficiencySpine["Compact generation, compression, fast generation, semantic representation, and resource budgets"]
  EfficiencySpine --> SubstrateSpine["Simulation, search substrates, Circle contracts, coil memory, and cyclic mixers"]
  SubstrateSpine --> EvidenceSpine["Executable specs, benchmarks, policy optimization, and artifact stewards"]
  EvidenceSpine --> IntegrationSpine["Integrated reference architecture, Theseus reference path, prototype roadmap, living method, and research backlog"]
  IntegrationSpine --> EvidenceLanguage
```

## Reading path

The shortest path through the book is:

1. **Why a stack:** ASI must be decomposed into governed layers before it can be made efficient, testable, or corrigible.
2. **How work moves:** human intent becomes command contracts, plans, context packets, claims, jobs, artifacts, and evidence records.
3. **How capability grows:** routing, compression, procedural memory, benchmarks, and stable capability fields let the system improve without erasing its safety boundary.
4. **How the book stays alive:** claims, proofs, schemas, tests, source notes, and public renders form the book's own evidence ratchet.

## How to read

Start with Part I for the architecture thesis, boundary model, and governance substrate. Read Part II as the operational spine: intent, planning, memory, reasoning, execution, and artifacts. Part III covers routing, compression, representation, mathematical substrates, and cyclic/proof-carrying mechanisms. Part IV explains evidence, implementation, Project Theseus as a reference path, and the living-book process.

The appendices are part of the architecture:

- Appendix A tracks source assignments.
- Appendix C tracks claim support states.
- Appendix D tracks protocol schemas.
- Appendix E tracks planned Codex tests.
- Appendix F tracks living updates.

## Evidence discipline

Every major claim should carry a support state. The default state is `argument`. Claims move only when sources are actually read, prototypes are inspected, proofs are checked, or tests are actually run. This book should never pretend that source ingestion or test evidence exists before it does.

## Living status

This release is a tagged v1.0.0 living-book baseline, not a finished v1.x evidence-and-reader release. The chapters are written end to end, and the repo validates scaffold, source-note, proof-readiness, schema, fixture, Lean, reading-mode, and render health locally. External-grounding placement is closed at 44/44 positioned chapters with 0 explicit exceptions, but deeper prior-art work, chapter-core evidence transitions, chapter-level tests, stronger safety-critical proof semantics, clean Project Theseus replay, stronger Circle replay or vendored contract-pack lanes, governed consolidation, and human-reader curation remain active roadmap items until their artifacts are added and recorded.
